*Recall note:* Highlights the negative consequences of traditional education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> We destroy this capacity above all by making them afraid, afraid of not doing what other people want, of not pleasing, of making mistakes, of failing, of being wrong.

*Narration:* Fear is instilled in children, hindering their natural learning capacity.

*Themes:* fear, compliance

*Recall note:* Highlights the detrimental effects of fear in education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> We encourage them to feel that the end and aim of all they do in school is nothing more than to get a good mark on a test, or to impress someone with what they seem to know.

*Narration:* School prioritizes grades over genuine learning.

*Themes:* test culture, compliance

*Recall note:* Critiques the focus on grades rather than understanding.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> We break down children's convictions that things make sense, or their hope that things may prove to make sense.

*Narration:* School undermines children's belief in logic and understanding.

*Themes:* boredom, institutional waste

*Recall note:* Addresses the negative impact of schooling on critical thinking.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.75]**

> We tell them, not what we think, but what we feel they ought to think.

*Narration:* Education often manipulates children's thoughts.

*Themes:* surveillance, compliance

*Recall note:* Highlights the lack of honesty in educational discourse.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> We think it our right and our duty, not to tell the truth, but to say whatever will best serve our cause.

*Narration:* Education prioritizes agendas over truth.

*Themes:* institutional waste, compliance

*Recall note:* Critiques the dishonesty in educational systems.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> We encourage children to act stupidly, not only by scaring and confusing them, but also by boring them.

*Narration:* Education fosters a culture of disengagement.

*Themes:* boredom, compliance

*Recall note:* Addresses the negative effects of boredom in schools.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> School tends to be a dishonest as well as a nervous place.

*Narration:* The school environment is fraught with dishonesty.

*Themes:* compliance, institutional waste

*Recall note:* Summarizes the toxic atmosphere of schools.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> We present ourselves to children as if we were gods, all-knowing, all-powerful, always rational, always just, always right.

*Narration:* Teachers often project an unrealistic image to students.

*Themes:* compliance, fear

*Recall note:* Critiques the unrealistic expectations set by educators.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> Children learn early that not to like school or the teacher is verboten, not to be said, not even to be thought.

*Narration:* Students are conditioned to suppress their true feelings about school.

*Themes:* compliance, fear

*Recall note:* Highlights the suppression of honest feelings in education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> What choked up and froze the minds of these children was above all else the fact that they could not express, they could hardly even acknowledge, the fear, shame, rage, and hatred that school and their teachers had aroused in them.

*Narration:* Students' emotional struggles are often ignored in schools.

*Themes:* fear, compliance

*Recall note:* Addresses the emotional toll of schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> What they found everywhere was what Silberman in Crisis in the Classroom called "appalling incivility" toward children on the part of almost all adults in schools.

*Narration:* Critique of adult behavior towards children in schools.

*Themes:* inequality, compliance

*Recall note:* Highlights systemic issues in school environments.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> There are enough examples to fill many books. I have mentioned one; no need to recite others here.

*Narration:* Acknowledgment of widespread issues in schools.

*Themes:* violence, fear, labeling

*Recall note:* Emphasizes the prevalence of negative experiences in schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> But these beatings, which the schools call "paddling" though many of them are brutal enough to send their small victims to the hospital, are only the official beatings.

*Narration:* Critique of physical punishment in schools.

*Themes:* violence, fear

*Recall note:* Illustrates the brutality present in school discipline.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> For every instance of physical brutality, there are many more of mental and spiritual brutality: sarcasm, mockery, insults-what Professor of Education Arthur Pearl calls "ceremonies of humiliation."

*Narration:* Discussion of psychological harm in schools.

*Themes:* mental health, fear, compliance

*Recall note:* Highlights the emotional toll of schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> The problem is not fake smiles and unmeant praise. It is far more serious than that--a widespread dislike, distrust, and fear of children so intense that it would not be off the mark to call it hatred.

*Narration:* Critique of adult attitudes towards children.

*Themes:* fear, compliance

*Recall note:* Addresses deep-seated issues in the school system.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

> We cannot have real learning in school if we think it is our duty and our right to tell children what they must learn.

*Narration:* Argument against coercive learning.

*Themes:* freedom, self-education

*Recall note:* Strongly supports the theme of self-directed education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> Fear is the inseparable companion of coercion, and its inescapable consequence.

*Narration:* Connection between coercion and fear in education.

*Themes:* fear, compliance

*Recall note:* Highlights the negative impact of coercive practices.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> The alternative--I can see no other--is to have schools and classrooms in which each child in his own way can satisfy his curiosity, develop his abilities and talents, pursue his interests.

*Narration:* Vision for a more liberating educational environment.

*Themes:* freedom, self-education

*Recall note:* Promotes the idea of personalized learning.

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> We made a terrible mistake when (with the best of intentions) we separated children from adults and learning from the rest of life.

*Narration:* Critique of the separation of learning from life.

*Themes:* inequality, freedom

*Recall note:* Calls for a reevaluation of educational structures.

---

## John Holt — *Instead of Education*

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Most people (particularly discerning parents) are convinced that our present educational system is not only mindless, but harmful as well. It damages children and ruthlessly suppresses their creative urges and individual identities. The most successful students are also the most dull and unimaginative, as efficiency in learning is exclusively identified with cramming.

*Narration:* Many discerning parents believe our current educational system is not just mindless, but harmful. It damages children, suppressing their creativity and individuality. The most successful students often turn out to be the dullest, as efficiency in learning is equated with cramming.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, creativity

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Education, with its supporting system of compulsory and competitive schooling, all its carrots and sticks, its grades, diplomas, and credentials, now seems to me perhaps the most authoritarian and dangerous of all the social inventions of mankind.

*Narration:* Education, supported by compulsory schooling and its grades and diplomas, appears to be one of the most authoritarian and dangerous social inventions.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> My concern is not to improve 'education' but to do away with it, to end the ugly and antihuman business of people-shaping and let people shape themselves.

*Narration:* I'm not concerned with improving education; I want to abolish it and end the harmful practice of shaping people, allowing them to shape themselves instead.

*Themes:* coercion, freedom

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The chances are we will have universal compulsory education and compulsory schools for at least another generation. Do not waste your energy trying to reform all these schools. They cannot be reformed.

*Narration:* It's likely that we'll have universal compulsory education for at least another generation. Don't waste your energy trying to reform these schools; they cannot be reformed.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> We need to reveal as untrue—as myths, illusions, and lies—the stories and alibis the schools and the educators tell us to justify themselves and explain their repeated failures.

*Narration:* We must expose the myths and lies that schools and educators use to justify their failures.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Thus, one of the stupidest things the S-chools do is insist that children 'comprehend' everything they read, and read only what they comprehend. People who read well do not learn to read this way. They learn by plunging into books that are 'too hard' for them, enjoying what they can understand, wondering and guessing about what they do not, and not worrying when they cannot find an answer.

*Narration:* One of the most misguided practices in schools is the insistence that children must fully comprehend everything they read, only engaging with texts they understand. In reality, proficient readers often dive into challenging books, relishing what they grasp, pondering the unknown, and not stressing over unanswered questions.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The most valuable and indeed essential asset the student brings to any learning task is a willingness to adventure, to take risks.

*Narration:* The most crucial asset a student brings to learning is their willingness to explore and take risks.

*Themes:* freedom, curiosity

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> If the child reads only when he wants to, and asks for help only when he feels he needs it, he will work at full capacity, throw himself into the task instead of away from it, and rarely need help at all.

*Narration:* When children read only when they choose to and seek help as needed, they engage fully with the task and seldom require assistance.

*Themes:* freedom, coercion

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> School is just this sort of compulsory-treatment institution. Society has decided that one group of people, teachers, shall do all sorts of things to another group of people, the students, whether they want it or not, until the teachers think the students measure up, know enough about the world to go out and live in it.

*Narration:* School functions as a compulsory treatment institution, where society has determined that teachers will impose their will on students until they believe the students are ready to face the world.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> In this community the children live their lives as they see fit. Subject only—like all of us—to the limits that they do not hurt each other, or destroy or unduly damage each others’ or common property, the children in this school do what they want, with whom they want, for as long as they want, and all of the time.

*Narration:* In this community, children are free to live their lives as they choose, limited only by the need to respect one another and their shared spaces.

*Themes:* freedom, curiosity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It is not possible to spend any prolonged period visiting public school classrooms without being appalled by the mutilation visible everywhere—mutilation of spontaneity, of joy in learning, or pleasure in creating, or sense of self. . . . Because adults take the schools so much for granted, they fail to appreciate what grim, joyless places most American schools are, how oppressive and petty are the rules by which they are governed, how intellectually sterile and esthetically barren the atmosphere, what an appalling lack of civility obtains on the part of teachers and principals, what contempt they unconsciously display for students as students.

*Narration:* Visiting public school classrooms for any length of time reveals a shocking reality—the mutilation of spontaneity, joy in learning, and creativity. Adults often overlook how grim and joyless most American schools are, governed by oppressive rules and an atmosphere that is intellectually sterile and aesthetically barren, where teachers and principals display contempt for students.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Compulsory and competitive schools are cruel by their very nature. . . . Many of them were badly hurt by the experience. To this day they have not recovered, and many never fully recover, from that school’s sustained (however unintended) attack on their dignity, independence, and self-esteem.

*Narration:* Compulsory and competitive schools are inherently cruel. Many children suffer deeply from their experiences, and some never fully recover from the attack on their dignity, independence, and self-esteem.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Even if it were true, as in some cases it may be, that S-chools today are somewhat less cruel, painful, fearful, and humiliating than they used to be, they are more harmful in other ways. They take much more of children’s time, and more all the time. They give children less and less time to live their own lives, pursue their own interests, or perhaps find ways outside of school to make up for the failures, fears, and boredom of school.

*Narration:* Even if schools today are somewhat less cruel, they are more harmful in other ways. They consume more of children's time, leaving them less opportunity to live their own lives and pursue their interests, often forcing them to compensate for the failures and boredom experienced in school.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> What is being asked for, and rewarded, and so trained, are fake smiles, fake laughter, fake teacher-pleasing behavior.

*Narration:* What schools demand and reward are fake smiles, fake laughter, and behavior that pleases teachers.

*Themes:* coercion, creativity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The children are not learning, never did learn, most of what the S-chools say they are T-eaching. The question we must ask, then, is what are they teaching, what are they really for? ... The first task is to shut young people out of adult society.

*Narration:* Children aren't really learning what schools claim to teach. We need to question what schools are actually doing. Their primary task seems to be keeping young people away from adult society.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> A much more important and indeed essential social function of S-chools is ranking—that is, grading and labeling, putting children into pecking orders, dividing them into winners and losers. ... The S-chools, then, must run a race which mostly rich kids will win but which most poor people will accept as fair.

*Narration:* Schools primarily serve to rank students, creating a divide between winners and losers. They set up a system where mostly affluent kids succeed, while the majority of poorer students are led to believe it's a fair competition.

*Themes:* testing, coercion

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Fear blocks learning. The skillful learner must trust the world, and himself to be able to cope with it. ... But whatever it is that happens, fear stops it from happening.

*Narration:* Fear hinders learning. To learn effectively, students must trust themselves and their environment. Yet, fear can prevent this essential process from occurring.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> S-chools say, and many S-chool people believe, that they really want all children to succeed, to learn all that the S-chools are trying to teach them. But if someday, somewhere, a T-eacher ever did the job he was paid to do, and got all his kids to learn all the stuff he was teaching, he would have to give them all A’s.

*Narration:* Schools claim they want every child to succeed, but if a teacher actually achieved that, they'd be pressured to raise standards and challenge students more.

*Themes:* testing, authority

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The S-chools, by taking the power to make choices from their students, corrupt them. It is as if, in making ourselves into interchangeable mechanical parts of institutions, we had transferred to them our very souls. The institutions live, hunger, thirst, suffer, and die. The people are robots.

*Narration:* Schools take away students' power to make choices, corrupting them in the process. It's as if we've turned ourselves into interchangeable parts of institutions, transferring our very souls to them. The institutions live and die, while the people become mere robots.

*Themes:* coercion, institutional_control

---

**[passage | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> They must not be jails for the young; in short, they must not, for any age, be compulsory. And they must not be allowed to rank and label their students.

*Narration:* Schools should not be prisons for the young; they must not be compulsory for any age, nor should they rank or label students.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It should be a punishable offense for a S-chool, or any of its governors, administrators, employees, consultants, or agents, to make, keep, or circulate such records about children.

*Narration:* It should be illegal for schools or their staff to create, maintain, or share records about children.

*Themes:* institutional_control

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> If the S-chool wants to give a child other sorts of tests—psychological, medical, or whatever, including IQ tests—they should be able to do so only with the parents’ permission, and must send them the results of those tests.

*Narration:* Schools should only administer psychological or medical tests with parental permission, and they must share the results with parents.

*Themes:* authority, institutional_control

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The S-chools have basically bad purposes; they cannot be made good places for children, for teaching, learning, intelligence, or growth, until those purposes are taken away from them; and these purposes cannot be taken away by people working in S-chools, but only by the general public.

*Narration:* Schools have fundamentally flawed purposes that cannot be transformed into nurturing environments for teaching and learning until those purposes are removed, and that change can only come from the broader public, not from within the schools themselves.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> To the extent they took us seriously they would say, 'Why do I have to sit here listening to you?'

*Narration:* If students took us seriously, they'd question why they have to sit and listen to us.

*Themes:* authority, coercion

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The idea of a 'radical teacher' is absurd.

*Narration:* The concept of a 'radical teacher' is fundamentally flawed.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> As long as S-chools remain S-chools—compulsory, coercive, competitive—any changes we make in them will not go very deep or very far, or last for very long.

*Narration:* As long as schools continue to be compulsory, coercive, and competitive, any changes we implement will be superficial and temporary.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Meanwhile, education—compulsory schooling, compulsory learning—is a tyranny and a crime against the human mind and spirit. Let all those escape it who can, any way they can.

*Narration:* In the meantime, compulsory schooling and learning represent a tyranny and a crime against the human mind and spirit. Let everyone who can find a way to escape it.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> It damages children and ruthlessly suppresses their creative urges and individual identities.

*Narration:* The current educational system harms children's creativity and individuality.

*Themes:* childhood, creativity, identity

*Recall note:* Strong critique of the educational system's impact on children.

---

**[quote | critical | medium | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> The most that may happen is that, once freed of the delusion that schools can solve these problems, we might begin to confront them directly, realistically, and intelligently.

*Narration:* Suggests that recognizing the limitations of schools can lead to addressing deeper societal issues.

*Themes:* inequality, institutional waste, reality

*Recall note:* Encourages confronting societal problems outside of the school system.

---

**[quote | critical | medium | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> We need to reveal as untrue—as myths, illusions, and lies—the stories and alibis the schools and the educators tell us.

*Narration:* Calls for exposing the false narratives surrounding education.

*Themes:* surveillance, compliance, truth

*Recall note:* Strong emphasis on debunking myths about education supports anti-school messaging.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

> You cannot have human liberty, and the sense of all persons’ uniqueness, dignity, and worth on which it must rest, if you give to some people the right to tell other people what they must learn or know.

*Narration:* Argues that true liberty cannot coexist with imposed learning.

*Themes:* freedom, inequality, dignity

*Recall note:* Strong philosophical argument against imposed education aligns with anti-school themes.

---

**[quote | critical | medium | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> Children do not need to be made to learn about the world, or shown how. They want to, and they know how.

*Narration:* Children's natural curiosity and desire to learn is emphasized.

*Themes:* childhood, self-education, curiosity

*Recall note:* Supports the idea of self-directed learning over formal education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> The principal flutist of the Boston Symphony under Kous-sevitsky used to say, “If I miss a day’s practice, I hear the difference; if I miss two days’, the conductor hears the difference; if I miss three days’, the audience hears the difference.”

*Narration:* The pressure to perform and the stress of expectations can be overwhelming.

*Themes:* work, stress

*Recall note:* Highlights the stress and pressure associated with performance.

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> But if he is allowed (as few children are) to continue to do it, to seek out in his own way and for his own reasons the meaning of written words, with only as much help as he may ask for; if this task which he has set himself isn’t taken from him and replaced with a lot of fragmented and meaningless tasks invented by someone else and done on their command; if he is not convinced by adults (as many children are) that he is not able to do this task he has set for himself, to figure out what written words say, but must “get” reading from a teacher as a patient gets a shot from a doctor; if he is very lucky, and none of these bad things happen, he will be reading well in a short time, perhaps even in a matter of months.

*Narration:* Children thrive when allowed to learn on their own terms, free from imposed tasks.

*Themes:* self-education, compliance, freedom

*Recall note:* Emphasizes the importance of self-directed learning and the negative impact of imposed tasks.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> Knowledge is a process in the minds of living people. It is what we do as we try to find out who and where we are, and what is going on about us.

*Narration:* Knowledge should be an active pursuit, not a passive reception.

*Themes:* self-education, freedom

*Recall note:* Encourages a view of knowledge as an active process, aligning with anti-school themes.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> The great majority of what we call schools are educator schools. They include all elementary, secondary, and other schools that people are required by law to attend.

*Narration:* Critiques the compulsory nature of traditional schooling.

*Themes:* compliance, institutional waste

*Recall note:* Directly critiques the compulsory nature of traditional schools.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

> Any school which is part of such a system of coercion is a S-chool.

*Narration:* Defines coercive schooling as fundamentally flawed.

*Themes:* compliance, institutional waste

*Recall note:* Strongly critiques coercive schooling systems.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> There can never be reality of encounter, truthfulness, honesty, when one person holds power over another.

*Narration:* Power dynamics hinder genuine communication.

*Themes:* power, truth, honesty

*Recall note:* Highlights the issue of power in educational settings.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> What they learn, most of the time, is only that we adults pretend, keep secrets, and tell lies.

*Narration:* Critiques the dishonesty in adult interactions with children.

*Themes:* truth, education, trust

*Recall note:* Emphasizes the negative lessons learned in school.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

> The school guesses—since it keeps no records on its students, it can only guess—that in its first four years, about two thousand people took its courses.

*Narration:* Points to the lack of accountability in traditional schooling.

*Themes:* inequality, education, access

*Recall note:* Shows the contrast between traditional and alternative education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> Since it charges no money, it does not need to guarantee anything to its students, and hence does not have to worry about the credentials or even the competence of its teachers.

*Narration:* Critiques the credentialism in traditional education.

*Themes:* institutional waste, education, access

*Recall note:* Challenges the necessity of formal qualifications.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.75]**

> But, like almost all public S-chool buildings, outside of school hours this one is tightly locked.

*Narration:* Critiques the underutilization of school facilities.

*Themes:* access, institutional waste

*Recall note:* Highlights the wastefulness of locked school resources.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.65]**

> What resources for do-ers do we now have, but need more of?

*Narration:* Questions the availability of resources for learning.

*Themes:* self-education, freedom, resources

*Recall note:* Encourages exploration of alternative learning resources.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> But such a process may make it very difficult to make these exchanges self-supporting in places where they are most needed, among poor people who have least access to S-chools and other expensive sources of information.

*Narration:* Access to educational resources is often limited for the poor.

*Themes:* inequality, access, education

*Recall note:* Highlights inequality in access to education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> Unlike S-chools, it does not say we must use it, or that bad things will happen to us if we don’t or wonderful things if we do.

*Narration:* Libraries offer a voluntary space for learning.

*Themes:* freedom, compliance

*Recall note:* Contrasts libraries with compulsory schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> Most people, having learned to dislike the things (including reading) they were made to do in S-chools don’t do them any more after they leave school, and so don’t use the library.

*Narration:* School can create a lasting aversion to learning.

*Themes:* boredom, dislike of school, self-education

*Recall note:* Addresses the negative impact of schooling on lifelong learning.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> Why should kids have to go full time to a S-chool just to be able to use the S-chool shop?

*Narration:* Questions the necessity of full-time schooling for access to resources.

*Themes:* freedom, access, education

*Recall note:* Challenges the structure of compulsory schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

> Poverty is not a reading problem and better reading won’t solve it, and it is a cruel lie to pretend that it could or will.

*Narration:* Critiques the misconception that education alone can alleviate poverty.

*Themes:* inequality, education, poverty

*Recall note:* Strongly critiques the limitations of education in addressing poverty.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

> If they had ways to speak to each other about their common concerns, and make these known to people outside, they might be much more unified in spirit, and politically and economically more effective.

*Narration:* Emphasizes the importance of community communication and empowerment.

*Themes:* community, empowerment, political action

*Recall note:* Supports themes of community and collective action.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> In many cities there are food buying cooperatives, whose members are able to save 20 or 30 percent on their food.

*Narration:* Food cooperatives can provide economic relief and community support outside traditional systems.

*Themes:* inequality, work

*Recall note:* Highlights alternative economic structures.

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

> Many more people might run and enjoy running if they could find places to do it.

*Narration:* Access to safe spaces for exercise is crucial for well-being.

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> In a society of doers, many more would be playing than watching.

*Recall note:* Promotes a culture of engagement and activity.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> No one can act or learn for another. The do-er must do the work himself.

*Narration:* Emphasizes the importance of personal agency in learning.

*Themes:* freedom, self-education

*Recall note:* Supports the theme of self-directed learning.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> How can there be a compact of trust when the student is not free to choose what he shall learn, or when, or how, or with how much and what kind of help?

*Narration:* This highlights the lack of autonomy in the learning process.

*Themes:* freedom, compliance, trust

*Recall note:* Emphasizes the absence of choice in education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> How can there be a compact of trust when the teacher may be obliged (as I once was), in order to keep his job, to do things that he knows will harm the student, destroy his confidence and ability to learn?

*Narration:* Critiques the ethical dilemmas teachers face in a system that prioritizes job security over student well-being.

*Themes:* inequality, compliance, trust, health

*Recall note:* Addresses the harmful impacts of institutional pressures.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> Or when the teacher is obliged, if the student does something poorly, to tell the whole world, to put it into a record which will follow the student all his life?

*Narration:* Highlights the long-term consequences of public labeling in education.

*Themes:* labeling, surveillance, stress

*Recall note:* Focuses on the damaging effects of permanent records.

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

> The task was ideal for still another reason. The car itself gave me the feedback and correction I needed.

*Recall note:* Shows how effective learning can occur in non-school environments.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.75]**

> The student, the do-er, can only learn a difficult action insofar as he can put the teacher inside himself.

*Recall note:* Encourages self-directed learning.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

*Themes:* self-education, freedom

*Recall note:* Promotes the idea of self-reliance in education.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

*Recall note:* Encourages the idea of fostering independence in learners.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.6]**

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.65]**

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> The work load for T-eachers in conventional S-chools is so heavy only because the S-chools and the T-eachers believe, and soon convince the children, that everything that is learned must be T-aught.

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.75]**

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> The tests S-chools give are not of this sort. S-chools give tests, as Winston Churchill said of his school, not to find out what you know but what you don’t know, and not so that they may help you find out what you don’t know, but only so they may say you are better or worse than other students.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> For when one person is in a position in which he can judge, and so reward or punish another, the other is almost certain to have at the center of his mind the thought, 'What does the judge want? How can I please him, or fool him? How can I best escape from this place of judgment, and hence of danger?'

*Narration:* Explores the psychological impact of judgment in schools.

*Themes:* fear, compliance, stress

*Recall note:* Addresses the fear and stress induced by school judgment.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> It is almost impossible for one person to see very far into the mind or thought of another, even under the best circumstances—and a S-chool, or any coercive institution like a S-chool, is the worst possible circumstance.

*Narration:* Critiques the limitations of understanding in coercive educational environments.

*Recall note:* Emphasizes the negative nature of coercive schooling.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.75]**

*Recall note:* Critiques the effectiveness of tests in understanding children's thoughts.

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

---

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.85]**

> People who learn, as children do in S-chool, to obey official authority out of fear of disgrace or punishment—sullenly, blindly, like Dr. Milgram’s subjects, irresponsibly—are almost certain to lose the ability either to recognize real and natural authority or to submit to it, willingly, responsibly, and with a whole heart.

---

---

---

---

## John Holt — *Teach Your Own*

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> I was struck by the difference between the 10s (whom I liked very much) and the 1s and 2s. The children in the classroom, despite their rich backgrounds and high I.Q.'s, were with few exceptions frightened, timid, evasive, and self-protecting. The infants at home were bold adventurers. It soon became clear to me that children are by nature and from birth very curious about the world around them, and very energetic, resourceful, and competent in exploring it, finding out about it, and mastering it.

*Narration:* I noticed a stark contrast between the ten-year-olds in my classroom and the infants at home. The classroom children, despite their advantages, were often frightened and timid, while the babies were bold adventurers. It became clear to me that children are naturally curious and capable explorers of the world.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, curiosity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> I had come to feel strongly that the kinds of changes I wanted to see in schools, above all in the ways teachers related to students, could not happen as long as schools were compulsory. Since compulsory school attendance laws force teachers to do police work and so prevent them from doing real teaching, it would be in their best interests, as well as those of parents and children, to have those laws repealed, or at least greatly modified.

*Narration:* I strongly believed that the changes I envisioned in schools, especially in teacher-student relationships, couldn't occur under compulsory schooling. These laws force teachers into roles akin to police, hindering genuine teaching. Repealing or modifying these laws would benefit everyone involved.

*Themes:* coercion, institutional_control

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Most of what I knew, I had not learned in school, or in any other such school like 'learning environments' or 'learning experiences'.

*Narration:* I realized that most of what I knew didn't come from school or any formal learning environments.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> These parents do not want their children in or anywhere near classes in which children learn what interests them most, for the satisfaction and joy of doing it. They want their children to believe what countless teachers and parents have told me: 'If I wasn't made to do things, I wouldn't do anything.'

*Narration:* These parents often resist the idea of their children engaging in classes that foster genuine interest and joy in learning. They instill the belief that without coercion, children wouldn't take initiative.

*Themes:* coercion, curiosity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> One reason people take their children out of school is that they think they aren't learning anything. In an article in the March 1978 issue of the Radcliffe Quarterly I pointed out that, with few exceptions, schools are appallingly incompetent at their work, even as they define it, having found it easier to blame all their failures on their students.

*Narration:* Many parents choose to take their children out of school because they feel their kids aren't learning anything. As I noted in a 1978 article, schools often fail at their core mission, preferring to blame students for these shortcomings.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, incompetence

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Though the courts have not yet agreed, compulsory school attendance laws, in and of themselves, seem to me a very serious infringement of the civil liberties of children and their parents, and would be so no matter what schools were like, how they were organized, or how they treated children.

*Narration:* While courts may not see it this way, I believe that compulsory school attendance laws fundamentally infringe on the civil liberties of both children and their parents, regardless of how schools operate.

*Themes:* coercion, civil_liberties

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It is nobody's proper business that a certain child got a certain mark in a certain course when she or he was eight years old.

*Narration:* It's simply not anyone's business what grades a child received at such a young age.

*Themes:* authority, civil_liberties

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> I have come not to feel obligated to rear and teach my children after any pattern or program devised, pre-scribed or presented by someone else or in such a manner as to meet any 'standards' set for them by any arbitrary political system. My children are well mannered, respectful to their parents, to their elders, to their peers, and to one another, are interested in life in general; they ask many questions which serve as the basis for their instruction one by one, day by day, and they appear articulate beyond their peers without their own family.

*Narration:* I've realized that I don't have to raise my children according to any prescribed patterns or standards set by arbitrary political systems. My children are well-mannered, respectful, and curious about life, asking questions that guide their learning every day.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Most people, however, take their children out of schools not so much for philosophical or political reasons as for the more direct and personal reason expressed by the Muslim mother: to prevent the schools from hurting their children, or hurting them any more than they already have.

*Narration:* Many parents withdraw their children from school not just for philosophical reasons, but to protect them from the harm that schools can inflict.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Many parents have written that their children's physical health improved strikingly once they were out of school. The changes that have occurred in Ishmael since we took him out of school have been unbelievable. Gone are the fits of temper that erupted every day around 4 P. M., gone are the headaches, the lines of tension around his mouth, and gone is his depression.

*Narration:* Many parents report significant improvements in their children's health after leaving school. For instance, Ishmael's transformation has been remarkable; his temper fits, headaches, and depression have all disappeared.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Putting children into a fixed or six-hour program at school is just squashing them. All the spirit and spunk they have when they are little is just wiped out.

*Narration:* When children are forced into a rigid six-hour school day, it stifles their spirit and creativity, wiping out the natural enthusiasm they have as young learners.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> In fact, most of what happens in school makes children feel the exact opposite--stupid, incompetent, ashamed.

*Narration:* In reality, school often leaves children feeling the opposite of empowered; they feel stupid, incompetent, and ashamed.

*Themes:* coercion, institutional_control

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> We think school can do something for us that we can't do ourselves.

*Narration:* Many believe that school offers something we can't achieve on our own.

*Themes:* authority, coercion

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> If you have the need, the answer is always there, too.

*Narration:* When there's a genuine need to learn, the answers will always present themselves.

*Themes:* curiosity, learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The fact is, however, that most schools are far more concerned to have children accept the values of mass society than to help them resist them. When school people hear about people teaching their children at home, they almost always say, 'But aren't you afraid that your children are going to grow up to be different, outsiders, misfits, unable to adjust to society?' They take it for granted that in order to live reasonably happily, usefully, and successfully in the world you have to be mostly like most other people.

*Narration:* Most schools prioritize having children accept the values of mass society rather than helping them resist those values. When educators hear about homeschooling, they often express concern that children might grow up to be outsiders or misfits, assuming that to thrive in life, one must conform to the majority.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> If there were no other reason for wanting to keep kids out of school, the social life would be reason enough. In all but a very few of the schools I have taught in, visited, or know anything about, the social life of the children is mean-spirited, competitive, exclusive, status-seeking, snobbish...

*Narration:* The social life in most schools is often mean-spirited and competitive. In fact, keeping kids out of school could be justified solely based on the negative aspects of their social interactions.

*Themes:* social_life, coercion

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The richer the families the children come from, the worse these traits seem to be.

*Narration:* Interestingly, it appears that the wealthier the families of the children, the more pronounced these negative social traits become.

*Themes:* coercion, social_life

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Children who spend almost all their time in groups of other people their own age, shut out of society's serious work and concerns, with almost no contact with any adults except child-watchers, are going to feel that what 'all the other kids' are doing is the right, the best, the only thing to do.

*Narration:* When children are isolated in peer groups and lack meaningful adult interactions, they often come to believe that whatever their peers do is the only acceptable behavior.

*Themes:* coercion, social_life

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> These are clearly not things that one has to spend three years talking about. And in fact these are not what schools of education talk about. They give very little thought to the act of teaching itself--helping another person find something out, or answering that person's questions. What they spend most of their time doing is preparing their students to work in the strange world of schools--which, in all fairness, is what the students want to find out: how to get a teaching job and keep it.

*Narration:* It's clear that these are not topics that require years of discussion. In fact, schools of education rarely focus on the essence of teaching—helping someone discover new knowledge or answering their questions. Instead, they primarily prepare students for navigating the peculiar environment of schools, which is what many students are eager to learn: how to secure and maintain a teaching position.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, institutional_control

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Scaring people only blocks learning.

*Narration:* Fear only hinders the learning process.

*Themes:* coercion, learning

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> A child teaching another is not disappointed if the other does not understand or learn, since teaching is not his main work and he is not worried about whether he is or is not a good teacher. He may be exasperated, may even say, 'Come on, dummy, pay attention, what's the matter with you?' Since children tend to be direct and blunt with each other anyway, this probably won't bother the learner.

*Narration:* When a child teaches another, they aren't disappointed if the other struggles to understand. Teaching isn't their primary role, and they don't stress about being a 'good teacher.' They might express frustration directly, but this honesty often doesn't deter the learner.

*Themes:* curiosity, learning

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> People do not need a Ph. D. or some kind of certificate to help their children find their way into the world.

*Narration:* You don't need a Ph.D. or a certificate to guide your children in navigating the world.

*Themes:* authority, coercion

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> There is in Michigan a man named Ovshinsky who stood solid-state physics on its ear by inventing a theory by which noncrystalline substances could be used to do things which, according to orthodox theory, only crystalline materials could do. For a number of years orthodox physicists dismissed Ovshinsky's ideas. But he was able to demonstrate them so clearly in laboratory experiments that they were finally obliged to admit that he was right. Ovshinsky never finished high school. There are probably more cases like this than we know, and there would be a great many more except for compulsory schooling laws.

*Narration:* In Michigan, there's a man named Ovshinsky who revolutionized solid-state physics with a theory that challenged conventional wisdom. Despite being dismissed for years, he proved his ideas through clear laboratory experiments, yet he never finished high school. This raises the question: how many more like him exist, hindered by compulsory schooling laws?

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It is a kind of Catch-22 situation to say, first, that all children have to spend all that time in schools, and then to say that all kinds of things can only be learned in schools.

*Narration:* It's a Catch-22: we say children must spend all their time in school, yet claim that only in schools can they learn.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Your business is, as far as you can, to help them realize their human potential, and to that end you put as much as you can of good into their lives, and keep out as much as you can of bad. If you think--as you do--that school is bad, then it is clear what you should do.

*Narration:* As a parent, your role is to nurture your children's potential by enriching their lives with positive experiences and shielding them from negativity. If you believe school is detrimental, the path forward is clear.

*Themes:* coercion, freedom

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> To learn to know oneself, and to find a life worth living and work worth doing, is problem and challenge enough, without having to waste time on the fake and unworthy challenges of school.

*Narration:* Learning to understand oneself and discovering meaningful work is challenging enough without the distractions of school’s trivial demands.

*Themes:* coercion, freedom

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Compulsory school attendance laws were invented by rich people and aimed at poor kids. These rich people said in effect, 'We educated people are perfectly capable of teaching our own children, but you poor people don't give a damn about your kids and don't know enough to teach them anything if you wanted to.'

*Narration:* Compulsory school attendance laws were created by the wealthy, targeting the poor. They essentially claimed, 'We can teach our kids, but you can't even care for yours.'

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> there's nothing special or even necessary about schools and that more children would probably be better off if they weren't forced to go. Our older son, a voracious reader then in the sixth grade, was bored in the classroom because almost everything that was taught he had already discovered on his own. For him school was a long review and an authoritarian prison which sapped his strength.

*Narration:* There's nothing special or even necessary about schools, and many children might be better off if they weren't forced to attend. Our older son, a voracious reader in sixth grade, found school boring because he had already discovered most of the material on his own. For him, school felt like a long review and an authoritarian prison that drained his energy.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Testing itself is not necessarily an accurate indicator of a person's knowledge or capabilities.

*Narration:* Testing isn't always an accurate measure of someone's knowledge or abilities.

*Themes:* testing

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The necessity for control and discipline outweighs the energy devoted to discovering and meeting each child's needs. A natural approach to children's learning does not force facts and skills on them before they are ready, but allows their own interests and talents to lead them into areas of knowledge.

*Narration:* The need for control and discipline often overshadows the effort to understand and meet each child's unique needs. A natural approach to learning doesn't impose facts and skills before a child is ready; instead, it lets their interests and talents guide them into new areas of knowledge.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Education is not something that should be done to you, but something that you do.

*Narration:* Education isn't just something that happens to you; it's something you actively engage in.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Learning goes on every hour of the day, so how can you grade or test that accumulated knowledge?

*Narration:* Learning happens all day long, so how can we possibly grade or test all that knowledge?

*Themes:* testing, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Once a child starts school, the home becomes school-centered, not family-centered.

*Narration:* When a child enters school, the focus shifts from family to school, making home less about family and more about education.

*Themes:* institutional_control, coercion

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Education is not something that should be done to you, but something that you do.

*Narration:* Education isn't just something that happens to you; it's something you actively engage in.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, freedom

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The school tries to match learning and individual development but this is impossible in a classroom of 20 students who all make demands on one teacher.

*Narration:* Schools attempt to align learning with individual growth, but this is unfeasible in a classroom filled with twenty students, each with their own needs.

*Themes:* institutional_control, coercion

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Learning goes on every hour of the day, so how can you grade or test that accumulated knowledge?

*Narration:* Learning happens continuously throughout the day, so how can we possibly grade or test all that knowledge?

*Themes:* testing, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> But the schools, and many adults outside of school, hardly ever recognize or honor such motives, can hardly even imagine that they exist. In their place they put Greed and Fear. How can unschoolers revive in their children those earlier, deeper, richer sources of human action? It is not easy. Perhaps the only thing to do is to be patient and wait. After all, if we do not constantly re-injure our bodies, in time they usually heal themselves.

*Narration:* Schools often overlook the natural curiosity and desire for mastery in children, replacing these with greed and fear. Unschoolers face the challenge of reviving these deeper motivations, requiring patience and faith in the healing of the human spirit.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> What children need is not new and better curricula but access to more and more of the real world; plenty of time and space to think over their experiences, and to use fantasy and play to make meaning out of them; and advice, road maps, guidebooks, to make it easier for them to get where they want to go (not where we think they ought to go), and to find out what they want to find out.

*Narration:* Children require access to the real world rather than just improved curricula. They need time and space to reflect on their experiences and to explore their interests freely, guided by their own desires.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, freedom

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> These truants rarely brush with the law. Their trademark is their solitude. ... They fear both worlds. They sense the futility of the jobs that are available even for those who do finish high school. ... Most of them are intelligent, sensitive children, far more accomplished in the arts of reading and mathematics than their peers who either attend the schools or lurk outside of them.

*Narration:* Truants often avoid trouble with the law, marked by their solitude and a deep awareness of the futility in both school and street life. Many are intelligent and skilled, often surpassing their peers in reading and math.

*Themes:* coercion, freedom

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> If this was so, it means that to punish him for cutting classes the school lied about her academic work--an outrageous and I would think, if tested in court, probably illegal practice which is common in schools all over the country.

*Narration:* Punishing students for skipping classes often leads schools to misrepresent their academic performance, a practice that could be deemed illegal if challenged.

*Themes:* testing, authority

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> How easy it would be, and how much less expensive than running giant schools, and jails for those who won't go to them, to find ways to help and encourage the interests and talents of these children, and many others like them.

*Narration:* Imagine the simplicity and cost-effectiveness of supporting children's interests and talents instead of maintaining large schools and jails for those who resist them.

*Themes:* freedom, coercion

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> But the school must not try to compel learning. If it does, people will argue endlessly and furiously about what kinds of learning must be compelled.

*Narration:* However, schools should not force learning. If they do, it leads to endless arguments about what should be required.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | provocative | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Any kid who has ever been to school knows that 'school' is only a special name for a kid's jail, and it's hard for them to imagine having fun, doing what they want, or being creative in jail.

*Narration:* Kids who have experienced school often see it as a kind of prison, making it difficult for them to envision fun, freedom, or creativity.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> What we need in our communities is not so much schools as a variety of protected, safe, interesting spaces where children can gather, meet and make friends, and do things together.

*Narration:* What our communities truly need are diverse, safe spaces for children to gather, connect, and engage in activities together.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, freedom

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Here I am talking about them learning without teaching --learning by doing, by wondering, by figuring things out, and often in the process resisting teaching when well-meaning adults try to force it on them.

*Narration:* In this section, I discuss how children learn without formal teaching—through doing, wondering, and figuring things out, often resisting attempts by well-meaning adults to impose teaching on them.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It's a waste of time and quickly degenerates into intellectual bullying to try to sidetrack a kid onto topics you think he should be learning.

*Narration:* Trying to force a child onto topics you believe they should learn is not only a waste of time, but it can also lead to intellectual bullying.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Some years ago I was reading aloud to a small child, as yet a nonreader, perhaps three or four years old.

*Narration:* I recall reading aloud to a small child, who was not yet reading themselves.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> A word to the wise is infuriating! Yes it is, because it is insulting, and little children pick up this expression of (often loving and protective) distrust or contempt, even when we're not conscious of sending it.

*Narration:* The phrase 'a word to the wise' can be infuriating and insulting to children, who sense the distrust or contempt behind it, even if we don't realize we're conveying that.

*Themes:* coercion, authority

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> Since to so many people 'learning' means what happens in school, or what is supposed to happen, I would rather use other words to describe what we humans do as a natural part of our living. 'Finding out' seems to fit pretty well.

*Narration:* Many equate learning with what occurs in school, but I prefer to describe our natural process of discovery as 'finding out.'

*Themes:* school_vs_learning

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> This little incident tells us more about the true nature of children (and so, all humans) than fifty years worth of Pavlovian behaviorist or Skinnerian operant conditioning experiments. Maybe Psychologist and Pigeon is a good game, for a while at least. But all human beings soon want to play Psychologist; no one wants to be the Pigeon. We humans are not by nature like sheep or pigeons, unquestioning, docile, happy to work the machine as long as it lights up its green lights or rolls out its food pellets. Like these children, we want to find out how the machine works, and then work it. We want to find out how things happen, so that we can make them happen. That is the kind of creature we are.

*Narration:* This incident reveals more about the true nature of children than decades of behaviorist experiments. While some may enjoy being tested, humans naturally want to understand and manipulate their environment. We are not passive like sheep or pigeons; we seek to discover how things work so we can make them happen.

*Themes:* coercion, curiosity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> It taught me an understanding of how the world works, so that I could figure out what I did not actually know. I recall what I did in fifth grade to free up more time to study airplanes, which I was then immersed in as a subject. The teacher wanted us to come up with five new words a week which we were supposed to define as a vocabulary lesson. Trouble was, words did not come to me at this steady pace. So, one day, I reached into the dictionary for two hundred-odd words and did a year's assignments in one bored stroke. Then I went back to gobbling up new and historical words as part of the new book I was writing on airplanes.

*Narration:* I learned how the world works, allowing me to identify gaps in my knowledge. In fifth grade, when tasked with defining five new words each week, I grew frustrated. So, I quickly completed a year's worth of assignments in one go, freeing myself to dive deeper into my passion for airplanes.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, curiosity

---

**[passage | critical | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> To parents I say, above all else, don't let your home become some terrible miniature copy of the school. No lesson plans! No quizzes! No tests! No report cards! Even leaving your children alone would be better; at least they could figure out some things on their own. Live together, as well as you can; enjoy life together, as much as you can. Ask questions to find out something about the world itself, not to find out whether or not someone knows it.

*Narration:* I urge parents not to let their homes turn into a miniature version of school. Avoid lesson plans, quizzes, tests, and report cards. Sometimes, leaving children to their own devices is better; they might discover things on their own. Focus on living and enjoying life together, and ask questions to learn about the world, not just to test knowledge.

*Themes:* coercion, school_vs_learning

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The most important question any thinking creature can ask itself is, 'What is worth thinking about?'

*Narration:* The key question for any thinking being is, 'What is worth thinking about?'

*Themes:* curiosity, freedom

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> When we deny its right to decide that for itself, when we try to control what it must attend to and think about, we make it less observant, resourceful, and adaptive, in a word, less intelligent, in a blunter word, more stupid.

*Narration:* When we take away the right to choose what to think about, we hinder observation, resourcefulness, and adaptability, ultimately making one less intelligent.

*Themes:* coercion, freedom

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> The reason my work with the leaves and worms was interesting and exciting to those boys was precisely that it was my work, something I was doing for my good, not theirs. It was not some sort of 'project' that I had cooked up because I thought they might be interested in it. I wasn't out there raking up leaves in the hope that some children might see me and want to join in. I never asked them to help, never even hinted; they instead on helping me.

*Narration:* The reason my work with the leaves and worms was so interesting and exciting to those boys was that it was my work, something I was doing for my own good, not theirs. It wasn't a project I created hoping they would be interested. I wasn't raking leaves hoping children would see me and want to join in. I never asked them to help, nor did I hint at it; they insisted on helping me.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, coercion

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> I hate to think of them ever becoming like the bored, sullen, angry, destructive teenagers who hang out every day at the Boylston Street entrance to the Public Garden.

*Themes:* coercion

---

**[quote | provocative | short | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> What you really mean is, you want to work on children. You want to do things to them, or for them--wonderful things, no doubt--which you think will help them. What's more, you want to do these things whether the children want them done or not.

*Narration:* What you really mean is that you want to work on children. You want to do things to them or for them—wonderful things, no doubt—that you believe will help them, regardless of whether the children want those things done.

*Themes:* coercion

---

**[passage | reflective | medium | pass=legacy | conf=1.0]**

> What they will teach is that there is no one best way, and that it is a waste of time and energy to look for it; that children (like adults) learn in a great many different ways; that each child learns best in the ways that most interest, excite, and satisfy her or him; and that the business of school should be to offer to learners the widest possible range of choices, both in what to learn and ways to learn it.

*Narration:* What they will teach is that there isn't just one best way to learn. It's a waste of time to look for it. Children, like adults, learn in many different ways, and each child learns best in ways that interest and excite them. Schools should offer the widest range of choices in what and how to learn.

*Themes:* school_vs_learning, curiosity, freedom

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.95]**

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

---

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.9]**

> I have come not to feel obligated to rear and teach my children after any pattern or program devised, pre-scribed (i.e., written beforehand,) or presented (i.e., sent ahead) by someone else or in such a manner as to meet any "standards" set for them by any arbitrary political system.

*Narration:* Rejecting imposed educational standards.

**[quote | critical | short | pass=recall | conf=0.8]**

> Many parents write to tell a story like this: Their child has taught himself to read, or somehow learned, before He went to school. He finds himself, perhaps in the pre-school, perhaps in one of the early elementary grades, reading from one to three years ahead of his class.

*Narration:* Highlighting the disconnect between self-directed learning and school demands.

*Themes:* self-education, compliance, test culture

---

**[quote | reflective | short | pass=recall | conf=0.7]**

> The experience of teaching my children has given me endless new insights concerning the role of parents (especially mothers), both what it is for most of us and what it could and should be, and the nature and meaning of education.

*Narration:* Emphasizing the transformative potential of parental involvement in education.

> There is in Michigan a man named Ovshinsky who stood solid-state physics on its ear by inventing a theory by which noncrystalline substances could be used to do things which, according to orthodox theory, only crystalline materials could do. But he was able to demonstrate them so clearly in laboratory experiments that they were finally obliged to admit that he was right. Ovshinsky never finished high school.
